title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh

tax_mentions.context: Section 3.5 notes UK carbon-tax acceptability varies with earmarking vs general budget; US polls report 43–45% support for a revenue-neutral carbon tax.
Section 3.5 summarizes Canadian choice-study where revenue recycling (VAT/income-tax rebates) increased acceptability of higher gasoline taxation.
Section 3.2 reports higher support for a fuel tax when people believe it reduces driving and benefits climate change.
tax_mentions.page_or_section: 3.2. Perceived benefits and effectiveness of policy

excluded_policy_mentions.reason_it_is_not_a_carbon_or_fuel_tax: Emissions trading/regulatory instrument for allowances; not a carbon/fuel tax or excise-like rate on fuel use.

inclusion_reason: Narrative review synthesizes empirical/experimental evidence on public support and acceptability for carbon and fuel taxes (e.g., CO2, carbon tax, gasoline and fuel taxes), including effects of perceived costs, fairness, and revenue recycling.
